era_context: 1739 was a year of significant geopolitical shifts. The Russo-Turkish War (1735–1739) concluded with the Treaty of Belgrade, establishing Russia as a major regional power and weakening Ottoman authority. In Europe, the War of Jenkins' Ear (1739–1748) between Britain and Spain began, reflecting colonial rivalries in the Americas. The Qing Dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor (r. 1735–1796) was in its early years of expansion and consolidation. The Mughal Empire in India was destabilized by Nadir Shah's invasion and the sack of Delhi. The Ottoman Empire faced internal administrative challenges and external military pressure.

events.title: Sack of Delhi by Nadir Shah
events.description: Nadir Shah, the Persian ruler, invaded the Mughal Empire in late 1738 and sacked Delhi in March 1739. The invasion resulted in massive destruction, looting, and loss of life. Nadir Shah's forces plundered the imperial treasury and the city, carrying away immense wealth including the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor diamond. The sack of Delhi marked a catastrophic blow to Mughal prestige and power, accelerating the empire's decline. The invasion destabilized the region and established Persian dominance over former Mughal territories.

sources.name: Mirza Mehdi Khan, History of Nadir Shah (contemporary Persian chronicle)
Mughal court records and later Indian historiography
European eyewitness accounts from merchants and diplomats

events.certainty_note: The sack of Delhi is well-documented in Persian, Mughal, and European sources. The date (March 1739) is confirmed in multiple contemporary accounts.

events.title: Treaty of Belgrade
events.description: The Russo-Turkish War (1735–1739) concluded with the Treaty of Belgrade, signed in September 1739. The treaty resulted in significant territorial gains for Russia, including the cession of Azov and other territories in the Black Sea region. The Ottoman Empire, weakened by the conflict, accepted Russian expansion and agreed to restrictions on Russian naval activity in the Black Sea. The treaty established Russia as a major regional power and marked a turning point in Ottoman-Russian relations, with Russia increasingly dominant.
events.certainty_note: The treaty is well-documented in diplomatic records and the text of the treaty itself survives. The date and terms are uncontested.

events.title: War of Jenkins' Ear Begins
events.region: Caribbean and Atlantic (British and Spanish colonial territories)
events.description: The War of Jenkins' Ear (1739–1748) began in October 1739 when Britain declared war on Spain. The conflict was ostensibly triggered by the incident in 1731 when Spanish coast guards severed the ear of British merchant captain Robert Jenkins, but the underlying cause was colonial rivalry in the Americas and the Caribbean. The war was part of the broader War of Austrian Succession and reflected European great-power competition for colonial dominance. The conflict involved naval battles, privateering, and colonial skirmishes.

sources.name: British parliamentary records and declarations of war
Contemporary British and Spanish naval records

events.certainty_note: The declaration of war and the date are well-documented in parliamentary and diplomatic records. The date is uncontested.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War Concludes
events.description: The Russo-Turkish War (1735–1739) formally concluded in 1739 with the Treaty of Belgrade. The war had been fought over control of territories in the Caucasus, the Black Sea region, and the Balkans. Russian forces, under the command of Peter Lacy and others, had achieved significant military successes. The treaty established Russia as a major regional power and weakened Ottoman authority in the region. The war's conclusion marked a shift in the balance of power in Eastern Europe and the Mediterranean.

sources.name: Russian military records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The conclusion of the war and the treaty are well-documented in Russian and Ottoman sources. The date is confirmed.

events.title: David Hume Publishes A Treatise of Human Nature
events.description: David Hume, the Scottish philosopher, published the final volume of his A Treatise of Human Nature in 1739. The work, which had begun publication in 1738, presented a comprehensive philosophical system based on empiricism and skepticism. Hume's Treatise was influential in the development of modern philosophy, particularly in epistemology and the philosophy of mind. The work challenged traditional metaphysical assumptions and established Hume as a major intellectual figure of the Enlightenment.

sources.name: A Treatise of Human Nature (primary text)

events.certainty_note: The publication date and content of the Treatise are well-documented. The date is uncontested.
cross_references: 1748-001 (Hume publishes An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of the War of Jenkins' Ear as being triggered by a single incident (Jenkins' ear) has been substantially revised by modern scholarship. Historians now emphasize that the war was primarily a manifestation of broader colonial and commercial rivalries between Britain and Spain, with the Jenkins incident serving as a convenient pretext rather than a genuine cause. The incident itself occurred in 1731, and the war was declared in 1739—an eight-year gap that suggests the ear was not the primary motivation. Additionally, the significance of Hume's Treatise was not immediately recognized; the work was initially poorly received and had limited influence during Hume's lifetime. Its later reputation as a foundational philosophical work developed over decades.
historiographic_note: 1739 is well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding military and political events. Russian, Ottoman, British, and Spanish diplomatic records provide rich detail on the treaties and wars. Indian sources (Mughal and Persian) document the sack of Delhi, though Persian sources may emphasize Nadir Shah's achievements while Mughal sources may downplay the extent of the disaster. Scottish sources document Hume's publication. Potential biases include: British sources may justify the War of Jenkins' Ear as a response to Spanish aggression; Spanish sources may present a different narrative; Persian sources may exaggerate the significance of the Delhi sack; Mughal sources may be incomplete due to the chaos of the invasion. The sack of Delhi is sometimes underrepresented in European histories, which tend to focus on European events.

graph_edges.note: The sack of Delhi destabilized the Mughal Empire, with effects extending into 1741
The Treaty of Belgrade concluded the Russo-Turkish War
The War of Jenkins' Ear continued until the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle in 1748
